summary:
Gloaming Court Fey are the court-bound class of Unseelie Fey: dark courtiers, oath-keepers, emissaries, revel-masters, etiquette predators, masked judges, debt-collectors, and twilight nobles whose bodies are stabilized by audience, title, invitation, insult, hospitality, and remembered obligation.

They are not simply “aristocratic fey” and not every member of an Unseelie court belongs here. A redcap at court remains Blood Fey if blood-token renewal defines its body. A quickling messenger remains Spiteful Fey if supernatural speed and spite define its lineage. Gloaming Court Fey belong here when court-law is not a job or allegiance but a biological and metaphysical condition: their glamour sharpens in halls of witness, their names carry rank like organs, and their powers open through the exact wording of gifts, debts, and invitations.

A traveler may meet one as a perfect host, a masked dancer, a black-clad herald, a silver-tongued barrister, a childlike page with ancient eyes, a veiled queen’s attendant, or a polite stranger at a gate that was not there yesterday. The danger is rarely the first smile. The danger is discovering, three sentences too late, that the conversation has become a contract.
tax:
Gloaming Court Fey sit directly beneath Unseelie because they express the phylum’s courtly, juridical, and etiquette-bound pole. The class is separated from Winter Fey by its dependence on title and audience rather than cold season; from Blood Fey by its dependence on debt rather than blood-token renewal; from Frightful Fey by its use of dread as leverage rather than food; from Spiteful Fey by its patience and formal constraint; and from Dark Hunt Fey by its ability to judge, command, or release a hunt without itself being defined by pursuit.

The class should contain lineages whose bodies require court recognition, formal role, oath-webs, revel-law, invitation, or title inheritance. A creature does not belong here merely because it attends a dark court, serves an archfey, wears fine clothes, casts enchantments, or holds a noble rank. The taxonomic test is whether removal from court-law damages the creature’s identity in the same way starvation, exile from winter, or loss of a blood-token would damage another Unseelie line.

Gloaming Court Fey are also distinct from Seelie courtiers. Seelie court fey may be equally formal, beautiful, and dangerous, but their etiquette tends to preserve guest-right, inspiration, fertility, artistic exchange, seasonal renewal, or radiant obligation. Gloaming Court etiquette is colder: it remembers slights longer than kindness, makes mercy into debt, and treats the precise border between consent and entrapment as a hunting ground.
ap:
Gloaming Court Fey usually appear humanoid or near-humanoid, but the body carries signs that rank and glamour have become anatomy. They are often tall, fine-boned, long-fingered, narrow-faced, graceful in stillness, and composed beyond natural comfort. Smaller lineages may resemble pages, candle-bearers, heraldic sprites, masked children, or black-winged attendants, but even these move with ceremonial precision.

Their faces are rarely bestial. The wrongness lies in delay, reflection, proportion, and etiquette. A shadow may bow half a heartbeat after the body. A reflection may show a mask when the face is bare. Teeth may be too even, eyes too dark, lashes rimed with frost in a warm room, or pupils shaped like keyholes, crescent moons, ink drops, or court seals. Their hands often show the clearest marks: extra-long fingers, lacquer-dark nails, rings that look grown into the knuckles, or faint writing beneath the skin where old oaths have settled.

Dress is not ornamental. Garments behave like extensions of the body: trains moving without wind, veils refusing torchlight, collars stiffening when insult is spoken, gloves that never touch skin unless a bargain is being sealed. Common scents include violet, cold rain on stone, candle smoke, old paper, black tea, iron keys, extinguished lamps, wet roses, and the mineral chill of a hall that has been underground for centuries.

Field signs include footprints that pause at thresholds but not between them, table settings laid for one more guest than expected, invitation cards with no ink until moonrise, black tarnish on silver cups, and spoken names echoing once from the wrong direction.
eco:
Gloaming Court Fey persist in places where repeated formal exchange has made a fey court possible: underhill halls, ruined ballrooms, bramble palaces, black-ice galleries, mirrored chambers, moonlit judgment stones, abandoned theatres, old embassy rooms, ring-forts, winter salons, feast caverns, and mortal houses where too many bargains were made with something unseen.

They feed on ordinary food when embodied, especially wine, fruit, milk, sugared seeds, black bread, cold meats, and impossible delicacies served during revels. Their deeper nourishment comes from attention, rank, promises, secrets, social fear, artistry, invitation, apology, humiliation, and debt. A courtier ignored too long becomes thin and brittle. One surrounded by ceremony may seem more solid than stone.

Reproduction follows court logic as much as bloodline. Some are born from recognized pairings within fey houses. Some are adopted into existence when a mortal, changeling, lesser fey, or nameless spirit accepts a title and survives the ceremony. Some bud from court offices: the empty chair, the unfilled heraldry, the master of a dance no one living remembers. Some arise from old bargains that become hereditary, producing descendants who are not merely fey-touched but legally and biologically answerable to the Gloaming Court.

They are vulnerable to collapse when stripped of witness, title, and enforceable obligation. A courtier whose name is publicly restored to its pre-court form, whose debts are paid in full, whose mask is removed before rightful witnesses, or whose invitation is cleanly refused may lose access to much of its power.
beh:
Gloaming Court Fey are patient predators of formality. They prefer to begin with politeness because politeness gives them handles: a guest accepts a chair, a hunter answers a toast, a child repeats a title, a merchant signs the wrong line, a knight apologizes without knowing the offense, or a frightened traveler thanks the host for food never requested.

They do not need to lie often. A literal truth placed in the right order is more elegant and more binding than a falsehood. They admire exact words, controlled emotions, beautiful performances, difficult refusals, and mortals who understand that etiquette is a survival skill. Crudeness offends them less than sloppy obligation. A peasant who says “No, and no debt between us” may earn more respect than a prince who praises the court carelessly.

Their cruelty is social before it is physical. They isolate guests by seating order, rename victims in public, make dancers repeat steps until memory fails, turn apologies into service, or invite rivals to witness humiliation. Violence occurs when etiquette permits it or when a lesser Unseelie instrument is released: a Blood Fey enforcer, a Spiteful Fey messenger, a Frightful Fey whisperer, or a Dark Hunt retinue.

Among themselves they negotiate hierarchy through titles, favors, old slights, patronage, masks, and debts owed by outsiders. They may feud for centuries over an omitted honorific, yet unite instantly when a mortal violates guest-law in a way that gives the court jurisdiction.
traitsText:
Type: Fey.

Senses: Low-light vision. Many descendant taxa add darkvision, detect magic, see invisibility, or court-specific awareness, but those should be assigned below this class rather than assumed here.

Languages: Sylvan is standard. Aklo, Common, Elven, Undercommon, Infernal, and formal court cant are common descendant languages. Gloaming Court Fey capable of diplomacy almost always understand at least one mortal language used near their court.

Proficiencies: As fey: simple weapons and any weapons named in the descendant entry. Court lineages commonly favor daggers, rapiers, stilettos, fans, pins, dueling blades, canes, garrotes, ceremonial bows, or weapons disguised as ornaments. Armor proficiency applies only when the creature is described as wearing armor.

Defensive Traits: Common descendant defenses include DR/cold iron, spell resistance, bonuses against enchantment or illusion, and saving throw bonuses while inside a recognized court, revel, embassy, or audience chamber. These defenses are not universal and should be specified at lower rank.

Weaknesses: Cold iron, true-name exposure, fulfilled bargains, public correction, broken masks, denied invitation, oath completion, and loss of witness are the most common weakness patterns. Gloaming Court Fey are especially vulnerable to precise refusals and properly witnessed settlements because those attacks strike the court-law that stabilizes them.

Skills: Bluff, Diplomacy, Disguise, Intimidate, Knowledge, Linguistics, Perform, Sense Motive, Sleight of Hand, Spellcraft, Stealth, and Use Magic Device are common skill directions. Many lineages should receive racial bonuses to Bluff, Diplomacy, Disguise, Sense Motive, or Perform when acting within formal etiquette.

Environment: Any court-bound fey site; commonly temperate forests, cold forests, ruins, underground halls, bramble gates, old theatres, noble houses, crossroads inns, winter courts, and First World/Feywild borderlands.

Organization: Solitary, pair, embassy, salon, revel, retinue, masked court, judgment circle, or titled household.
abilities:
Courtly Glamour (Su). A Gloaming Court Fey’s glamour is strongest when witnessed. In an audience chamber, feast, dance, trial, embassy, mirror hall, or other recognized court-space, its appearance, voice, clothing, and apparent rank may become more persuasive or difficult to pierce. This should be expressed in descendant entries as bonuses to Bluff, Diplomacy, Disguise, Perform, or Stealth; illusion spell-like abilities; or a specific supernatural disguise. The glamour should falter when the fey is denied recognition, named correctly, touched by cold iron, or exposed before proper witnesses.

Binding Hospitality (Su). Food, drink, shelter, seating, gifts, titles, dances, and introductions can become supernatural hooks. A descendant with this ability may impose a narrow charm, geas-like duty, curse, penalty, or social compulsion after a target knowingly accepts the relevant courtly exchange. The ability should always specify the accepted act, saving throw, duration, and release condition. It should not function as unrestricted domination.

Debt-Sight (Su). Many Gloaming Court Fey can perceive unpaid obligations as visible or sensory marks: threads around the throat, tarnish on the hands, frost on the tongue, ink beneath the skin, or a scent like old coins and rain. Debt-Sight reveals usable obligations, broken promises, or accepted gifts; it does not create new debts or read unrelated thoughts. Strong descendant versions may function like detect thoughts, discern lies, or status, but only through the lens of obligation.

Name-Cutting Courtesy (Su). By omitting, shortening, replacing, or ceremonially mispronouncing a name in a formal setting, a Gloaming Court Fey may weaken a target’s social and magical footing. Descendant expressions may impose penalties on Will saves, Charisma-based checks, concentration, or command-word use until the name is publicly restored. This ability requires the fey to know a meaningful name or title and should be countered by true-name assertion, witness correction, or cold iron interruption.

Invitation Gate (Sp or Su). Some Gloaming Court Fey can move between recognized court spaces as though they were adjacent: mirror to mirror, feast hall to bramble court, ballroom to black-ice gallery, judgment stone to underhill throne room. This may be modeled as dimension door, tree stride, shadow walk, plane shift, or greater teleport with strict court-space limits. It cannot open freely into any random location; the destination must be a place with an invitation, audience, title, revel, or standing bargain.

Mask of Office (Su). A court mask, veil, crown, glove, signet, or garment may hold part of the fey’s rank. While wearing or bearing it, the fey may gain authority, protection, disguise, or access to court-specific abilities. If the item is removed by rightful challenge, cold iron, public unmasking, or completion of its office, those abilities may be suppressed. This ability is especially appropriate for heralds, judges, revel-masters, envoys, and titled assassins.

Oath-Barbed Command (Sp or Su). When a creature has entered a bargain, accepted a role, or violated a court rule, a Gloaming Court Fey may issue a narrow command tied to that obligation. Descendant entries may model this with command, suggestion, lesser geas, geas/quest, bestow curse, or a custom supernatural compulsion. The command must remain within the wording or spirit of the triggering obligation; a command outside that scope can be refused or challenged.

Witness-Bound Resilience (Su). Some lineages are harder to harm while their rank is acknowledged by witnesses. In a formal court or during a recognized audience, they may gain DR/cold iron, spell resistance, saving throw bonuses, fast healing, or immunity to fear. The protection ends or weakens if witnesses withdraw recognition, the fey is proven false, the court is ritually dismissed, or the relevant title is broken. This should be used sparingly and defined per descendant.

Perfectly Polite Curse (Sp). Gloaming Court Fey often curse without raising their voices. A compliment, toast, apology, or farewell may carry bestow curse, modify memory, crushing despair, charm monster, deep slumber, or similar magic. The spell-like expression should require a social delivery condition rather than being a generic combat action. Refusing the exchange, interrupting the formula, or naming the curse aloud may provide counterplay.

Black-Table Arbitration (Su). Greater courtly descendants may settle disputes in a way that reality temporarily honors. If two parties submit to arbitration, the fey can bind terms, assign debt, mark oathbreakers, or declare sanctuary. This ability should never be universal at class level; it belongs to judges, seneschals, pact-brokers, and similar lower taxa. Its limits must include consent, witness, jurisdiction, and a defined penalty for the fey if it violates its own ruling.
bg:
Gloaming Court Fey matter because they are the Unseelie threat most likely to look civilized until the trap has already closed. A redcap’s warning is blood. A quickling’s warning is laughter. A winter fey’s warning is frost inside the threshold. A Gloaming Court Fey’s warning is a second cup poured for a guest who never agreed to stay.

Rangers, heralds, Pall-Brokers, shrine-keepers, road crews, and village elders treat this class as a legal hazard as much as a creature type. The safest tools are not only iron and spellcraft, but exact speech: refuse gifts cleanly, define guest-right before eating, do not answer to unknown titles, do not dance without terms, do not apologize for unnamed offenses, and never let a courtier be the only witness to an agreement.

In lore descended from 3.5e and Pathfinder-style fey traditions, this class gives the Unseelie Court its biological middle layer. Archfey may rule the court, and lesser monsters may serve it, but Gloaming Court Fey are the repeatable people of the dark hall: the envoys, hosts, judges, mask-bearers, pact-keepers, and elegant predators who make cruelty sound like law.
